title: Using discrepancy theory to improve the design of randomized controlled trials
speaker: Daniel Spielman
abstract: In randomized experiments, such as a medical trials, we randomly assign the treatment, such as a drug or a placebo, that each experimental subject receives. Randomization can help us accurately estimate the difference in treatment effects with high probability. We also know that we want the two groups to be similar: ideally the two groups would be similar in every statistic we can measure beforehand.

proof_stages.label: Potential-outcome setup and Horvitz-Thompson error
proof_stages.summary: The talk formalizes randomized controlled trials using fixed Neyman-Rubin potential outcomes, defines designs as random treatment partitions, introduces the average treatment effect and the unbiased Horvitz-Thompson estimator, and derives the estimator error as an inner product with the outcome-sum vector.

proof_stages.label: Variance criterion and baseline designs
proof_stages.summary: Writing the variance as a quadratic form involving the assignment covariance matrix Z, the speaker analyzes IID and balanced designs to show that IID is minimax unless one assumes structure in the potential outcomes, while balance can help when the outcome-sum vector has a large mean component.

proof_stages.label: Covariates as a matrix-discrepancy objective
proof_stages.summary: If the outcome-sum vector is close to the row span of the covariate matrix X, the variance is controlled by XZX^T, so the design problem becomes finding randomized signings that keep both Z and the covariate discrepancy small.

proof_stages.label: Gram-Schmidt walk signing construction
proof_stages.summary: The Gram-Schmidt walk is introduced as a randomized signing algorithm for input vectors, and its alive/frozen coordinates, pivot choice, update direction, and zero-mean step distribution are described.

proof_stages.label: Sharp one-sub-Gaussian analysis
proof_stages.summary: The speaker explains the improved analysis proving the optimal sigma-squared equals one, using the martingale structure of the updates, projection norm bounds, Hoeffding's lemma, phase-by-phase control, and an operator-monotone matrix inequality.

proof_stages.label: Augmented-covariate tradeoff theorem
proof_stages.summary: By feeding augmented vectors combining coordinate directions with covariates into the Gram-Schmidt walk, the parameter phi trades robustness against covariate balancing and yields Loewner-order, operator-norm, variance, and sub-Gaussian tail guarantees, interpretable as a ridge-regression-type bound.

proof_stages.label: Optimality and hardness barriers
proof_stages.summary: Trace and identity-matrix arguments show the main variance bound cannot generally be improved, and an NP-hardness result of Charikar, Newman, and Nikolov rules out guaranteed instancewise improvements beyond this scale.

proof_stages.label: Confidence intervals and practical deployment
proof_stages.summary: The talk constructs conservative variance estimates using Horvitz-Thompson ideas and Neyman's diagonal trick, explains why random permutation helps with pairwise inclusion probabilities, and then discusses tighter confidence intervals, covariate transformations, regression adjustment, class-size and online variants, rerandomization, and practical choices of phi.

examples.name: Flipped-classroom trial with old and newborn subjects
examples.note: The classroom intervention, with old people and newborns as observable covariate groups, makes potential outcomes and covariate imbalance concrete without being used in the proof.

examples.name: All-positive outcomes under balanced assignment

examples.engagement_reason: the speaker uses the balanced-design variance formula to reason that when all outcomes are positive, subtracting the mean of mu can reduce the norm and make balancing preferable
